sections.heading: WHO Classification
sections.content: Small cell lung cancer (SCLC) is defined as a poorly differentiated malignant epithelial tumor categorized as a high-grade neuroendocrine carcinoma [SCL-B]. According to the World Health Organization (WHO) classification system, SCLC is distinguished from other neuroendocrine tumors—particularly typical and atypical carcinoids—due to significant differences in epidemiology, genetics, treatment, and prognosis [SCL-B, MS-5]. SCLC is characterized by rapid doubling time, high growth fraction, and early development of widespread metastases [MS-3].

sections.heading: Histologic Features
sections.content: On histology, SCLC cells are described as small blue cells with scant cytoplasm, high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio, fine granular nuclear chromatin, and absent or inconspicuous nucleoli [SCL-B]. The cells are round, oval, or spindle-shaped with molding and high mitotic counts [SCL-B]. An important distinguishing feature from large-cell neuroendocrine carcinoma (LCNEC) is the high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio and paucity of nucleoli in SCLC [SCL-B].

sections.heading: Staging
sections.content: The staging of SCLC uses a combined approach of the American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) TNM staging system and the historic Veterans Administration (VA) two-stage classification [MS-6]. Limited-stage SCLC is defined as stage I–III (T any, N any, M0) that can be safely treated with definitive radiation doses, excluding T3–4 due to multiple extensive lung nodules or tumor/nodal volume too large to be encompassed in a tolerable radiation plan [Table 1]. Extensive-stage SCLC is defined as stage IV (T any, N any, M1a/b/c) or T3–4 with characteristics precluding safe radiation delivery [Table 1].

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.content: Approximately 95% of small cell carcinomas originate in the lung, though they can also arise from extrapulmonary sites including the nasopharynx, gastrointestinal tract, and genitourinary tract [MS-3]. The most useful characteristics for distinguishing SCLC from typical and atypical carcinoids are the mitotic count: SCLC has more than 10 mitoses per 2 mm² field, atypical carcinoid has 2–10 mitoses per 2 mm² field, and typical carcinoid has 0–1 mitoses per 2 mm² field [SCL-B].
